structured.summary: This paper proposes LID, a framework that leverages pre-trained language models (LMs) as policy initializers for sequential decision-making tasks in embodied environments. It demonstrates that fine-tuning LMs on sequential embeddings of goals, observations, and histories improves task success rates and combinatorial generalization, both with expert data and via an active data gathering procedure without expert demonstrations.

strengths.point: Novel use of pre-trained language models as general policy initializers for sequential decision-making beyond language tasks.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.2 and throughout the paper describe initializing policies with pre-trained LMs and fine-tuning them for decision-making.

strengths.point: Demonstrated significant improvement in combinatorial generalization on challenging embodied tasks.
strengths.evidence: Section 6.1.1 reports a 43.6% improvement in task completion rates on novel tasks in VirtualHome compared to baselines.

strengths.point: Integration of an active data gathering procedure enabling policy learning without expert data.
strengths.evidence: Section 4.2.2 and Section 6.2 describe LID-ADG, which uses exploration, hindsight relabeling, and policy updates to learn from failures.

strengths.point: Comprehensive analysis of factors contributing to generalization, including input encoding, sequential representation, and weight initialization.
strengths.evidence: Section 7 presents ablation studies showing that sequential input and pre-trained weights are important, while natural language encoding is not strictly necessary.

weaknesses.point: Reliance on hand-designed rules for hindsight goal relabeling in active data gathering.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 9 and Appendix F.2 acknowledge that the active data gathering procedure depends on hand-crafted relabeling functions.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on scalability and computational cost of fine-tuning large pre-trained LMs in embodied environments.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper mentions using GPT-2 but does not provide detailed analysis of training time or resource requirements beyond brief notes in Appendix D.2.

weaknesses.point: Experiments are limited to two environments (VirtualHome and BabyAI), which may restrict generality claims.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5 and experiments focus on these two environments; broader evaluation on other domains is suggested but not performed.

weaknesses.point: No released code or data at the time of submission, limiting reproducibility.
weaknesses.evidence: Checklist notes that code and data will be released after acceptance.

weaknesses.point: The approach may inherit biases from pre-trained LMs, but the paper only briefly mentions this without empirical investigation.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 9 mentions potential bias issues but does not analyze or mitigate them.

weaknesses.point: The active data gathering method is evaluated only on tasks with single predicate goals, limiting assessment on more complex tasks.
weaknesses.evidence: Appendix G.2 states that active data gathering relabels only short sub-trajectories with single goal predicates.

weaknesses.point: The impact of different LM architectures or sizes is not explored.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper uses GPT-2 but does not compare with other LMs or larger models like GPT-3.

questions.question: How sensitive is the method to the quality and design of the hindsight relabeling function in active data gathering?
questions.motivation: Understanding this sensitivity is important for applying LID-ADG to new environments where hand-designed relabeling may be difficult.

questions.question: Can the approach scale to more complex tasks with longer horizons and more predicates in the goal, especially in the active data gathering setting?
questions.motivation: Current evaluation focuses on relatively short sub-tasks; scaling to complex tasks is critical for real-world applicability.

questions.question: Have the authors considered or tested the impact of using larger or different pre-trained language models on performance and generalization?
questions.motivation: Exploring different LM architectures could reveal whether improvements are due to model size or pre-training corpus.

questions.question: What are the computational costs and training times for fine-tuning the LM-based policies compared to baseline methods?
questions.motivation: Practical deployment requires understanding resource requirements and efficiency.

critique_points.point_001: Reliance on hand-designed rules for hindsight goal relabeling in active data gathering. (Section 9 and Appendix F.2 acknowledge that the active data gathering procedure depends on hand-crafted relabeling functions.)
critique_points.point_002: Limited discussion on scalability and computational cost of fine-tuning large pre-trained LMs in embodied environments. (The paper mentions using GPT-2 but does not provide detailed analysis of training time or resource requirements beyond brief notes in Appendix D.2.)
critique_points.point_003: Experiments are limited to two environments (VirtualHome and BabyAI), which may restrict generality claims. (Section 5 and experiments focus on these two environments; broader evaluation on other domains is suggested but not performed.)
critique_points.point_004: No released code or data at the time of submission, limiting reproducibility. (Checklist notes that code and data will be released after acceptance.)
critique_points.point_005: The approach may inherit biases from pre-trained LMs, but the paper only briefly mentions this without empirical investigation. (Section 9 mentions potential bias issues but does not analyze or mitigate them.)
critique_points.point_006: The active data gathering method is evaluated only on tasks with single predicate goals, limiting assessment on more complex tasks. (Appendix G.2 states that active data gathering relabels only short sub-trajectories with single goal predicates.)
critique_points.point_007: The impact of different LM architectures or sizes is not explored. (The paper uses GPT-2 but does not compare with other LMs or larger models like GPT-3.)
critique_points.point_008: How sensitive is the method to the quality and design of the hindsight relabeling function in active data gathering? (Understanding this sensitivity is important for applying LID-ADG to new environments where hand-designed relabeling may be difficult.)
critique_points.point_009: Can the approach scale to more complex tasks with longer horizons and more predicates in the goal, especially in the active data gathering setting? (Current evaluation focuses on relatively short sub-tasks; scaling to complex tasks is critical for real-world applicability.)
critique_points.point_010: Have the authors considered or tested the impact of using larger or different pre-trained language models on performance and generalization? (Exploring different LM architectures could reveal whether improvements are due to model size or pre-training corpus.)
critique_points.point_011: What are the computational costs and training times for fine-tuning the LM-based policies compared to baseline methods? (Practical deployment requires understanding resource requirements and efficiency.)
